skill.name: auth-implementation-patterns
skill.description: Master authentication and authorization patterns including JWT, OAuth2, session management, and RBAC to build secure, scalable access control systems. Use when implementing auth systems, securing APIs, and designing permission models.
skill.summary: Build secure authentication and authorization systems with JWT, OAuth2, sessions, and RBAC patterns
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Educational documentation skill containing authentication and authorization code patterns. All 67 static findings are false positives: backticks are markdown code fences, environment variable access demonstrates proper secret handling, and weak crypto mentions are in cautionary context. No actual security risks present.

content.value_statement: Learn battle-tested authentication and authorization patterns to build secure access control in your applications without reinventing the wheel.
actual_capabilities: Generate secure JWT authentication flows with access and refresh tokens
Implement session-based authentication with Redis storage
Design OAuth2 social login with Google and GitHub
Create role-based access control (RBAC) authorization systems
Build permission-based and resource ownership authorization
Apply security best practices including rate limiting and password hashing
limitations: Does not provide working code that can be copied and run directly
Does not include backend server setup or database configuration
Does not cover MFA or biometric authentication implementation
Frontend UI components for login pages are not included

use_cases.title: Build JWT authentication from scratch
use_cases.description: Implement complete token-based auth with access tokens, refresh tokens, and proper secret management
use_cases.target_user: Backend developers building new authentication systems

use_cases.title: Add OAuth2 social login
use_cases.description: Integrate Google and GitHub OAuth2 authentication into existing applications

use_cases.title: Design authorization model
use_cases.description: Create RBAC or permission-based access control systems for application resources

prompt_templates.title: Basic JWT Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to implement JWT authentication in Node.js with Express, including token generation and verification middleware

prompt_templates.title: Refresh Token Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a refresh token flow that securely stores refresh tokens in a database and issues new access tokens

prompt_templates.title: OAuth2 Integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Implement Google OAuth2 login using Passport.js with JWT token generation after authentication

prompt_templates.title: RBAC Authorization
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a role-based access control system with admin, moderator, and user roles, including middleware for permission checking

output_examples.input: How do I implement JWT authentication in Express?
output_examples.output: A complete JWT implementation includes: 1) Generate tokens using jwt.sign() with secrets from environment variables, 2) Create authenticate middleware that verifies Bearer tokens, 3) Use short-lived access tokens (15min) with longer refresh tokens (7d), 4) Store refresh tokens hashed in database. See implementation-playbook.md Pattern 1 for full code.

output_examples.input: What's the difference between session and token-based auth?
output_examples.output: Session-based: Server stores state, session ID in cookie, simple but requires sticky sessions. Token-based (JWT): Stateless, self-contained claims, scales horizontally, but cannot revoke individual tokens easily. Choose sessions for traditional apps, JWT for APIs and microservices.

output_examples.input: How do I implement role-based authorization?
output_examples.output: Define roles in an enum (USER, MODERATOR, ADMIN), create a role hierarchy mapping, build requireRole() middleware that checks user role against allowed roles, apply middleware to protected routes. Example: app.delete('/users/:id', authenticate, requireRole('ADMIN'), handler)

best_practices: Always use environment variables for secrets (JWT_SECRET, SESSION_SECRET) never hardcode credentials
Use short-lived access tokens (15-30 minutes) with separate refresh tokens for better security
Store refresh tokens hashed in database and implement token rotation on use
anti_patterns: Storing JWT in localStorage exposes tokens to XSS attacks - use httpOnly cookies instead
Not validating token expiration allows expired tokens to be used indefinitely
Client-side only authorization checks can be bypassed - always validate server-side

faq.question: When should I use session-based vs token-based authentication?
faq.answer: Use sessions for traditional server-rendered apps where you need simple logout and CSRF protection. Use tokens (JWT) for SPAs, mobile apps, and microservices where you need stateless authentication and cross-domain requests.

faq.question: How do I securely store JWT secrets?
faq.answer: Never hardcode secrets in source code. Use environment variables (process.env.JWT_SECRET) and load them from .env files at runtime. Ensure .env is in .gitignore and secrets are injected from secure sources in production.

faq.question: What's the difference between authentication and authorization?
faq.answer: Authentication (AuthN) verifies WHO a user is (login with credentials). Authorization (AuthZ) determines WHAT a user can DO (permissions, roles). Both are essential but serve different purposes in access control.

faq.question: How do I implement refresh tokens securely?
faq.answer: Generate refresh tokens as random UUIDs, store hashed versions in database, set expiration (7-30 days), implement rotation (issue new refresh on each use), and allow revocation for logout or security events.

faq.question: Can JWT tokens be revoked?
faq.answer: JWTs are stateless by design. To revoke: use a token blocklist in Redis/database, implement short expiration so tokens expire naturally, or use refresh token rotation to invalidate old token pairs.

faq.question: What's the best way to handle password storage?
faq.answer: Always hash passwords using bcrypt or argon2 with high work factors (bcrypt cost 10-12). Never store plain passwords. Use salt automatically provided by bcrypt. Verify with bcrypt.compare().
